speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Birkenau.

speaker: interviewee
text: It started? Well, I was in this [? C ?] lager. And many of the things I simply won't be able to tell about because there's just so much time.

speaker: interviewer
text: But tell us about [? C ?] lager that you want to. Everything you want to, tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were there. I was there with my sister. And we tried to take care of each other as much as possible. And what we did was my sister, who's the older one, so we kind of pooled our resources. Anything I got I usually gave her. And she is the one who doled it out. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What you got, you mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if I got something over what we both got.

speaker: interviewer
text: Food, is that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Food. That's-- no, it wasn't only food actually, because we just had these-- these thin little dresses, nothing else. And I look like such a nebechel. It was-- you see, when there was a curfew, you couldn't go to toilets. You to go through the back, steal yourself out of-- of the barracks. And that was not far from the electrified wires. And after that, there was a road and then other electrified wires, which you found out that what the Sonderkommando was-- was. Sonderkommando are the people who work the crematorium, men. And they got all kinds of things. And many of the women on our side, you know, were young girls. And-- and some of them knew how to-- how-- how to make up to men. It was over a road and two electric wires, of course. And they talking to them, and said, my name is such and such. And they said, my name is such and such. They were able to send over some things, throw over the wires. So they got all kinds of things, these-- these girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like clothes? What do you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Even clothes, because we were always cold. Or some bread or some jelly, I think, I might have gotten. All kinds of things.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I sometimes went to the toilet that way. I was too shy to-- to-- to even look anywhere. And at one point, one man said, I want her, pointing to me. And of course, I did-- assumed it was someone else, of course. And so a girl would come, you want me? You want me?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, no, no, I want her. And I looked like such a nebechel. He chose the biggest nebechel and insisted he wanted me. And I said, finally, finally, they had to bring me there. Because I-- I didn't believe he wanted to talk to me. Well, turned out he wanted to talk to me because I looked really rather-- I didn't look like someone who could take care of herself so well, and he simply wanted to help. And he told me, and told me the truth. He-- he was-- his name Shlomo. And he was about 27. And he came from Poland. He wrote me letters, you know, sent it over with a-- you put it around a-- a rock and then you can throw it more easily. You know, letters that were difficult to throw because it's paper. He told me all kinds of things about himself. He came with a-- a wife and a child. Of course, they were taken away. And he's working there. And anything I would like and he has it to ask for it. And if it's-- if I want to go to someone-- in other words it wasn't for me because he loved me in any way. He told me that-- he asked me who I have there. And I said, sister. So he says, well, fine. It's for her, too. And if you don't need it, you give it to some-- someone else. He simply wanted to help. That's all. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you write back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and I wrote back.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get paper? Where did you paper?

speaker: interviewee
text: He sent me. He sent me pieces of paper and pencil. And he's the one-- apparently didn't really have so many things, because he sent me over a jacket, which is very important, and not two jackets. He knew that my sister needs one. We-- between the two of us, we had a jacket, which was quite important. And then my sister decided to wear, you know, beautiful shoes to come there. And they got torn, you know, was gone. So he sent over a pair of shoes, which was a very important. And food from time to time, which was most important. And told me when to come. And he'd throw it over there. And, of course, my sister got everything I had. There wasn't enough to give to other people, to tell you that much. So he probably didn't have as much as all that, because I was the only one who-- to whom he gave it. It wasn't like to give it to everybody. He didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: So apparently, there was just a limited amount that he could. But he did send me over. And that helped. That helped a great deal. Did you have any other function in the-- in the lager?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I suppose that too had something to do with my nebechel looks, [LAUGHS] because the blockalteste, who was the head of the block, who was a tall, terrible-- a crazy woman, really. I don't if she was as vicious as crazy after all those years in camps. She chose me to be the gatekeeper, which meant that when it was curfew, which was most of the time, really, if anybody had to go out, I should open the gate. And then anybody who wanted to come to let them in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they go out to where? Where would they go?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the toilet. Usually to the toilet. I mean, you couldn't confess you want a drink. To toilet. And for that I got-- I got an extra ration of soup, which was a-- a real important thing. So I got some things more. From Shlomo, as I said, I got some things, but certainly not to-- to have to my fill, certainly not. Anyway, we were there about nine weeks, which made that May, June-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] towards the end of July. We-- of course, I forgot to mention the fact that they have all kinds of selections. They selected-- meaning they had those zahlappells, which was the counting. And then an important looking German, most the time it was Mengele, who came to see if there are anybody-- if there's anybody who doesn't look very strong and take out all the ones who weren't-- who looked very weak and just take them away. And after awhile, we began thinking this is last journey. Well, at one point, they took out my sister, because she had swollen feet and she wasn't feeling so well. Boy, I-- it was just the most terrible thing. They kept them somewhere surrounded. And somehow or other, I got her out. I got her out. I don't know what I would have done. Anyway, I got her out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So any time there was a selection, she wanted to stand behind me, because usually we faced the German, hoping he'll see me. He won't see her because I was thinner than she before the war. But I lost less weight than she did. Like after the war, I still looked all right. And she was on the heavy, actually.

speaker: interviewee
text: She weighed about 80 some pounds. She was shorter than I, you remember. And I weighed almost 90, which was-- I am the same height as I am now. No, taller because I went down already. [LAUGHS] And-- so we knew what the kind of a selection of it is. And all of a sudden, they were making another selection. The way you knew what kind is whom they selected. If you see all kinds of weak people were selected, you tried to get out of it. And then all of the sudden we see a selection where they take only the tall, healthy looking people. So we figured it must be a good thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when they came to my row, they selected me and not my sister. And I said, but I have a sister, a sister. And surprise, surprise, instead of saying, you know, like they other times said, so what? And just ignored you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at this point, they said, OK, let your sister come with you. And she came also. And they took us to a certain place in the barracks and-- with the perennial showers and washing. And-- and we were given new clothes. Well, we had the-- the clothes that we were given at first-- were given prison-- prisoner's clothes-- because they were taking us somewhere. And they took us by regular train, regular train.

speaker: interviewer
text: With seats inside?

speaker: interviewee
text: With seats inside, and SS in it also, of course. But regular trains, as a matter of fact, it was a civilian train, different cars. But it was a civilian train. It stopped at stations.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the prisoner's clothes were with the stripes? Or what were the prisoner's--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just gray. Gray. I think the men had the stripes. We always had gray. And they took us to work in Reichenbach, which is a city in Germany. And I think I was looked it up where it is exactly. I'll have to do it again because I can't remember. It was in Germany, where they have a-- had a telephone factory, where they were making parts for-- for airplanes, we were told. And they took us to a small little camp surrounded by civilians, as a matter of fact. They weren't supposed to talk to us or anything. And we couldn't talk to them either. But a small camp, where we found some women already from-- from Holland. And some Dutch women came with us also.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these were all Jewish women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. All Jewish women. And we were taken to work to a factory, usually very early morning because we had to start probably at 6 o'clock. We didn't have watches. But took more than an hour to get there by foot. And we worked 12 hours in well-lighted, warm place. It was very, very nice. And there was some Polish, non-Jewish Polish, people also there working, young women and-- and men who were taken away from their families from Poland. And they were not in prison, but they were also living in barracks. And they had much more food, had their own clothes and all that. And there were some Germans also. And I sat next to a German woman, next to two German women. We weren't allowed to talk to each other. But they ignored. There was another German woman at another table who did not talk, not only that, but she was spying on the others. But these two women talked to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: In German?

speaker: interviewee
text: In German.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, it's interesting. I knew German quite well. I don't know any now. Anyway, so they told me that when we came in they thought we were brought from an insane asylum, because without hair with these long dresses-- you know, they didn't try to fit you with a dress. They just handed you a dress-- they thought we were brought from an insane asylum. They were afraid. They didn't know what was happening.

speaker: interviewee
text: But later on, I asked one of them. I said, well, didn't you have Jewish neighbors? She says, yes. Well, what happened to them? They took them somewhere. They didn't pay much attention where and what, or so she said.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, these two German women were very nice. And they were on rations also, you know. They didn't have much food. They gave me things. They really did. They gave me-- once I got an apple, which you never heard of in those days. And I got some candy once. There's one of them, was quite young, and she didn't use her ration cards very wisely. She ate it all up all at once. I have an idea that then she had boyfriends. So they took care of her. And-- but she gave me things. And the other one also. Also, they had soup also for-- for lunch. But theirs was so much better. So they gave me that, which was all illegal.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they fed you in the middle of the day there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, lunch, or at night, in-- at midnight. We worked one week nights and one week in the day time. Anyway, we were there surrounded by beautiful gardens and everything. We couldn't go out or anything. And we went to work every day except for Sunday. And we had a little more food. And we were given bunk beds, where each person slept separately with straw for a mattress, which was heaven, and a-- and a blanket, and a straw pillow, so which was really heaven. And then towards the winter, they brought us coats. The coats that were taken from prisoners-- you know, our own coats. And as a matter of fact, there was one young woman who found her coat. And since it was such a beautiful one, they didn't want to believe her that was hers, because it's one way of getting a beautiful coat. But I happen to know her from home. And I told them it was her coat. I remember when she had it made-- a beautiful coat. And she did get it. They just hand out this, this, this, and so on. But she did her coat. And here again things didn't fit. So it was too long, shortened it, all kinds of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had needles and thread to shorten things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's it. In the factory, there were some Polish girls. And one Polish girl-- by the way, I really had a lot of luck-- one Polish girl decided I was her sister. She said she never had a sister and I'm her sister, which meant that she gave me a needle, thread. Whatever I asked her for, she gave me. She give me a toothbrush and-- and toothpaste. Whatever I asked her, she said if I can it bring it, I'll-- I'll do so, which was illegal, again illegal, everything illegal. Anyway, so we were there, working like this, August, September, October, November, December, about five months, five and a half months. And the 1st of January, when it was so cold, it was terrible cold, we were evacuated. We heard-- and this-- this is something really quite interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: My friends who was-- who were with me tell me that there was bombardment. And that's why we were taken away. I can't for the life of me remember that bombardment. And they said to me, I talked to them, don't you remember we were taken down into the shelters? I says, I know nothing. I can't remember at all. And honest, I can't really remember everything. And even though they remind me, I couldn't-- I still can't remember that part.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just remember that we were taken away in a march. We began marching. And deep snow, you could hardly walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: January '45?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was January '45. We were walking. And, of course, our clothes wasn't very good-- oh, oh, we were given two rations of bread. And that's it. And this went on for some days. And anybody who couldn't walk, just was-- stayed behind. And we heard some shots. I never saw. And if we had just heard some shots, so we knew we'd better not stay behind. And my sister, again, developed [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] swelling in her feet. And she couldn't walk. And we were five in a row. We kind of pushed and pulled her and so on. She made it. But we knew that you stay behind and that's it. And every night, they would take us to-- to some sheds and sleep there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many-- approximately how many were you on this march?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some hundreds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hundreds?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, some-- let's say about 500. There was another camp not too far away, who also worked in this factory. But they went separately. And this after some days-- at that time, we met some other people also, those people--

speaker: interviewer
text: The SS men were accompanying you on-- on by foot too? They march as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: By foot, by foot, with dogs and guns and women, who came and they saw someone talking, gave them a zetz, a real slap. I once got a terrible slap. I almost fainted because I dared to talk to someone. Anyway, so we walked for five, six, seven days. I don't know. Finally, we came to an abandoned camp-- well, a few times we came to abandoned camps. But finally we were put on cattle cars. But this time with snow falling below zero weather, instead of having the closed cattle cars, we had open cattle cars with the snow falling on us and, of course, no covering or nothing. And on each car, there was an SS man. So we just went around-- I don't know where we went. I know at one point we were in-- in Berlin. I know at one point, we were there. And it was all bombarded. And we stopped some places. We weren't given food. We gave-- before we got onto railroad, they give us a whole bread each. But then we didn't get anything for-- for weeks. And this is very crowded and very cold. We couldn't sit again. So the best place was in a corner. So if you sit, you have something to lean against. Because in the middle if you sit, it's very difficult to keep straight. Finally, after having stopped in many camps overnight, we came to a big camp. By that time, we had lots of other transports. They were thousands of people by that time. We came to a camp-- it was-- must've been at least March because it was beginning to be spring. We came to a camp called Porta Westfalica.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you know, here again in retrospect, why on earth was it so important for those Germans to give up railroad space, cars, manpower to schlep us from place to place when all they have to do is abandon us? And by that time it was the-- the-- the British who would have freed us. They didn't want that. They just schlepped us from place to place. And-- and really that they-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] it was to the disadvantage of their war effort. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Oh, boy. It was a terrible thing-- they must have forgotten to disconnect.

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Try talk-- you can tell us more over the--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So they gave up all this time and space and equipment and manpower just to schlep us from place to place. And finally, we came to Porta Westfalica. And it was going to be spring already. So we were able to find some-- some gray and try and cook it, because there's very little food. By the way, during all this time, there was no organized feeding. There just was no such thing. There were thousands of people. So you heard a rumor that over there somewhere, they're handing-- passing out soup. By time you got there, there wasn't any. Or sometimes you did make it. And once, I-- I made it, and I got some soup. And by that time, we had a transport of-- of gypsies. And the gypsies got hold of me and took my soup away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you any other encounters with them, with gypsies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Towards the end, I-- I met some gypsies. By the way, I still can't understand this business of the gypsies having been killed. I'm sure that there were many killed. But the town I come from, there were three kinds of gypsies. One kind became just wondering gypsies. And two kinds, one kind was living outside the city, who ate all kinds of-- they-- they just begged. And a third kind who lived with the rest of the population and tried to deny they're gypsies. Three kinds, nothing happen to them. They stayed right there. No one ever took them anywhere. So I don't know which gypsies were taken, because I do know that there were gypsies, because I encountered them. But certainly, they were not as anxious to-- to exterminate them like-- as the Jews. Absolutely not Jew. And that's such an accepted thing. Everybody knows the Jews and the gypsies. But it just isn't so, because in my town, I go back now and I can find the same gypsies. I don't find the same Jews. Anyway, so they took it away from-- from me. And that was that.

speaker: interviewer
text: They knocked it out of your hand? Or what did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, they just grabbed it away. Took it away. Anyway, so we were there in Porta Westfalica. It was getting spring already. And it was getting to be more organized, where we get some food, not as regularly. But we get some food. And that's where we put on Pesach as a matter of fact.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know about holidays while you were in camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the fact is there were some older people who somehow figured it out. And they told us it's Pesach. And before that they, told us it's Hanukkah or whatever. So we knew, but we tried not to eat bread the same evening. But of course, we ate. But we knew. And then it was a very big camp. And apparently, there was also a factory. So those people who had experience in the factory were taken there to-- to work. And I was one of them. It was a factory in the mountain. We had to-- we had to go down in an elevator at least 10 floors. Down, down, down, you just were inside the earth. And I did approximately the same kind of work. But not everybody had work at that point. As a matter of fact, most people didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as-- as a worker, did you have more privileges with food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I got the same food. Well, in the factory at lunch, I would get-- be sure to get the soup. And over there, not necessarily. So yes, I suppose there was something. Anyway, we were there. We were getting used to the place already. Of course, you adjust to so many things. So we adjusted. And of course, we didn't even think about of our families. We didn't know. They were probably also in some kind of a camp. Then towards the very end of April, must have been, or the very beginning of May-- let's say the 1st-- they unloaded us onto trains. And lo and behold, this time regular trains, regular trains-- with the SS guarding us. And we were going somewhere. Didn't know where. Oh, but I don't know at what point, we came to one camp. We must've stopped somewhere, where on the walls it said, this is your last station. On the walls, all kinds of writing, this is your last station. Don't expect to live after this. Wherever they take you, this will be the last you'll know. While we were on those trains for maybe a day, and we ended up in Hamburg. All right, I'll tell you about the rest after.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks.
